title: Magnetic influence on ion transport in concentrated solid solutions: An analytic investigation
abstract: It is well established that magnetic fields have a significant effect on transport in certain classes of electronic conductors. Less reported, however, are similar effects in solid ionic conductors. Despite the rarity of Hall mobility measurements in ionic conductors, recent experimental work in batteries and other systems has demonstrated that an applied magnetic field can significantly and beneficially alter ionic transport and electrochemical processes in solid materials in a way that would not be predicted from naïve Hall coefficient estimates. In this work, the influence of a magnetic field on ion transport in solids is investigated analytically, and general multi-component transport equations accounting for magnetic effects are presented. Specific models are then derived for solid, isotropic binary and single ion conductors. Material property combinations for which magnetic field influence may become significant are then computed for certain systems subject to compositional constraints. Finally, it is demonstrated that the derived model for binary conductors in a magnetic field fits experimental magneto-resistance data well for the fluoride ion conducting solid Pb$_{0.66}$Cd$_{0.34}$F$_2$, provided an assumption of near degenerate multi-component transport.
summary: This paper provides a new analytical framework for understanding how magnetic fields affect ion transport in complex solid electrolytes. By moving beyond single-carrier models to a multi-component binary conductor model, the authors can explain anomalous magnetoresistance observations in materials like Pb0.66Cd0.34F2. This work highlights the importance of accounting for species coupling in the presence of external magnetic fields.
main_problem: The lack of a rigorous, thermodynamically consistent analytical theory to explain how magnetic fields significantly influence ion transport in concentrated solid solutions.
main_result: The derivation of general multi-component transport equations accounting for magnetic effects, which successfully fits experimental magnetoresistance data for the fluoride ion conductor Pb0.66Cd0.34F2.
method: An analytical investigation using the Onsager-Stefan-Maxwell formalism, Langevin equations, and mathematical tools like the Sherman-Morrison-Woodbury relation and Lie derivatives.
model_or_system: Concentrated solid solutions and solid ionic conductors, including specific models for single ion conductors, binary conductors, and site-constrained systems (e.g., LLZO, ionogels, and Pb0.66Cd0.34F2).
key_observables: Ionic conductivity, magnetoresistance, Hall coefficient, Hall mobility, and species flux.
important_parameters: Magnetic field strength (b), electric field (Es), species charge (zi), concentration (ci), and the binary coupling parameter (eta).
main_assumptions: Assumes isotropic transport tensors, neglects magnetoresistance in high carrier concentration regimes, and assumes the magnetic field does not significantly alter fundamental interaction tensors.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the sensitivity of conductivity and Hall angle to Hall mobility across various magnetic field strengths; Figure 2 illustrates the sensitivity of the conductivity ratio to Hall mobility and binary coupling.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of magnetic influence on ion transport, moves through the derivation of general multi-component transport equations using electromagnetic-chemical potentials, presents specific models for various conductor types (single-ion, binary, constrained), validates the model against experimental magnetoresistance data, and concludes with a discussion on the need for multiphysics continuum models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Sensitivity of the conductivity to the Hall mobility Rhκ at different field strengths b = |b|. If g⊥is the component of the driving force perpendicular to b, the magneto resistance factor (1 + R2 hκ2b · b)−1 is the reduction in conductivity in the direction of g⊥(left). The Hall angle θh = arctan(R1 hκ|b|) is the angle between the resultant current in the direction of g⊥and in the direction perpendicular to both g⊥and b (right). Note that the Hall coefficient may be negative, so the Hall angle plot is rotated 180 degrees about the origin when Hall mobility is negative.

Figure 2: Sensitivity of the ratio κeff/κ⊥ eff, where κ⊥ eff is the coefficient of P ⊥ b in the conduc- tivity tensor, to the Hall mobility Reffκeff and binary coupling η = hmod/κeffReff at different field strengths b = |b|.

Figure 3: Sensitivity of the hall angle to the effective Hall mobility κeffReff and binary coupling η = hmod/κeffReff at different field strengths b = |b|. This is defined in the same way as before, only now it depends on the ratio [κeffReff −hmod] |b|/ [1 + κeffReffhmod(b · b)].
